summary: Jeff Atwood revisits a 1994 'Futurist Programming' manifesto that applied early 20th century Futurist art principles to software development, arguing against dogma and waste in programming. The manifesto rejected accepted practices like modularity, standards, and object-oriented methodologies in favor of speed and efficiency. Atwood notes that some predictions, like the rise of dynamic code generation, proved prescient. However, he warns that the core philosophy of rejecting dogma can itself become dogmatic, using the uncomfortable historical fact that the original Futurists were fascists as a cautionary note. The post advocates for critical examination of 'best practices' while acknowledging that some conventional wisdom remains useful.
key_points: The 1994 Futurist Programming manifesto rejected dogma in software development, calling for programs that prioritize user time and memory over programmer convenience
Hardware improved 200x over 20 years while programming practices stagnated since the invention of the subroutine
The manifesto's embrace of dynamic code generation anticipated the rise of dynamic programming languages a decade later
Critical examination of accepted wisdom is valuable, but skepticism of best practices can be taken too far
The original Futurist art movement's connection to fascism serves as a warning about ideological extremism
money_quotes: We react against the heavy religious atmosphere that surrounds every aspect of computer programming.

Computer hardware has realized performance increases of a factor of more than 200 in the last 20 years, while in program design very little progress has been made at all since the invention of the subroutine.
Fearing and attacking technology has become almost second nature to many people today.
Anything that is done for the convenience of the programmer at the expense of the user.
The core futurist philosophy that religion and dogma of all kinds should be examined critically is an important one.
You have to be careful that rejecting dogma doesn't itself become a kind of dogma.
Too bad they were all Fascists.
key_insight: Questioning programming dogma and best practices is healthy, but anti-dogmatism taken to extremes becomes its own rigid ideology.
